content.text: Before installing Cowork and creating your first Project, two things need to be in order: a compatible Anthropic account plan, and a supported operating environment. Getting these wrong is the most common reason a new user can see the Cowork desktop app but not certain features — or discovers mid-project that a connector or scheduled task is not available on their current tier. This topic maps out exactly what each plan provides and what hardware, software, and network conditions are required.

attrs.alt: Cowork plan tiers: Pro (individual), Team (shared projects), Enterprise (org-wide deployment)
attrs.title: Cowork Plan Tiers Comparison

content.text: Cowork requires an Anthropic account — the same account used for Claude.ai. There is no separate signup. What differs between plans is the feature set available once you sign in.
— Basic access to Claude chat. Projects are not available. Cowork-specific features including connectors, scheduled tasks, and the desktop app require at minimum a Pro subscription.
— Monthly subscription. Unlocks Projects, persistent memory, file upload, sandboxed code execution, web browsing, and the Google Drive connector. Suitable for individual knowledge workers.

— Per-seat subscription with a minimum seat count. Adds team-shared Projects, Slack and GitHub connectors, scheduled tasks, basic admin console, and usage reporting. Required for any multi-person Cowork deployment.
— Custom contract. Adds enterprise SSO (SAML 2.0 / SCIM), full audit logs, data residency options, custom retention policies, priority support SLA, and a dedicated account manager.
Purchasing Pro but expecting to share a Project with a colleague. Project sharing requires Team. An individual Pro user has private Projects only — they are not visible to other users.

Cowork is available in two forms: the desktop application and the browser-based web interface at claude.ai. Both serve the same features for most users, but the desktop app offers tighter integration with the host OS — including file system access grants and local notifications.

Linux users can access full Cowork functionality via the browser — all Project, connector, and scheduling features are available there. The desktop app's advantage over the browser is the macOS/Windows file system grant dialog, which lets Cowork read local files without manual upload.
The web interface at claude.ai requires a supported modern browser. Cowork uses WebSockets for live task streaming, so browser versions more than 18 months old may not work reliably.
Safari 16+ (macOS Ventura+); some file-upload behaviors differ

Cowork is a cloud-hosted service — all AI computation runs on Anthropic's infrastructure, not locally. The local machine only runs the desktop app (an Electron-based wrapper) or a browser tab, so hardware requirements are modest.
8 GB+ (for large file uploads and browser tabs)

The desktop app is built on Electron, which carries a baseline memory footprint of roughly 200–400 MB. On machines with less than 8 GB of RAM, keep other Electron-based apps (VS Code, Slack) in mind — they share the same memory budget.

Cowork is a cloud service: every inference call, connector action, and file read/write goes through Anthropic's infrastructure. Network quality directly affects response latency and the reliability of long-running tasks.
— 5 Mbps download / 2 Mbps upload. Sufficient for text-based tasks and small file uploads.
— 25 Mbps+ for large file uploads (PDFs, spreadsheets to Projects) and parallel subagent tasks that stream multiple responses simultaneously.
— Response latency is dominated by model inference time (typically 5–30 seconds for a full response), so network latency under 200 ms round-trip does not meaningfully affect the user experience.

. WebSocket upgrades on the same port are also required for live task streaming. Corporate firewalls that strip WebSocket headers or impose aggressive TLS inspection can cause tasks to stall mid-stream.

— Cowork has no offline mode. Without network access, the desktop app opens but displays a connection error and no inference or connector calls can complete. Scheduled tasks queued to run while offline are skipped, not retried.

Connectors are one of Cowork's most-used features, but not all connectors are available on all plans. The table below shows availability as of the time of writing — check the Cowork settings panel for the current list on your account.

Each connector also requires its own OAuth authorization. The connector being available on your plan does not mean it is connected — you still need to go through the connector setup flow (Settings → Integrations → Connect) to grant Cowork access to each service. The OAuth token is scoped to the minimum required permissions: for example, the Slack connector requests only the ability to read channel history and post messages, not admin-level access.

Enterprise deployments have additional setup steps that require IT or administrator involvement. These are not self-service — they require coordination with Anthropic's enterprise team before the contract is signed.

Anthropic provides SAML 2.0 metadata that your identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace) needs to register. After configuration, users sign in with their corporate credentials instead of a personal Anthropic password.

Automates user lifecycle: when an employee joins, they get a Cowork seat; when they leave, their account is suspended. Configured in your identity provider's SCIM app settings, pointing at Anthropic's SCIM endpoint with a provisioning token provided during onboarding.

Enterprise contracts can specify that customer data (conversation history, Project content) is stored in a particular region (US, EU). This must be chosen before the organization is created — it cannot be changed after the first user has onboarded.
Enterprise security teams may need to add Anthropic's CDN and API domains to their firewall allowlists. Anthropic provides a current list of CIDR ranges and domain names during onboarding.
Designate at least two admins during onboarding. Admins control which Projects exist, who can create connectors, and what usage limits apply to individual teams.
Upgrade to Pro (or Team/Enterprise for team sharing)
Connector requires Team or Enterprise plan
Upgrade plan, or use Google Drive connector (Pro-available)
Ask IT to allowlist wss://*.anthropic.com on port 443
Launch with --disable-gpu flag; update display drivers
Pop-up blocked by browser or OS
Allow pop-ups from claude.ai in browser settings
Upgrade to Team, or trigger task manually from Pro
Use this checklist before deploying Cowork to a team. Individual users can skip items marked [Team/Enterprise].
Confirm the correct subscription plan is active in Settings → Billing.
Verify macOS 12+ or Windows 10 22H2+ on all target machines (for desktop app).
and verify WebSocket upgrade succeeds (use browser devtools → Network → WS tab).
[Team/Enterprise] Add all users to the workspace and assign roles (member, admin).
[Team/Enterprise] Complete at least one connector OAuth flow end-to-end (Drive or Slack) to validate redirect URLs.
[Enterprise] Configure SSO and verify a non-admin user can sign in via the identity provider.
[Enterprise] Confirm data residency region with the Anthropic account team before first user onboarding.
Run a test task: create a Project, attach a file, and ask Cowork to summarize it. Verify the response arrives without errors.
Usage Limits and Rate Limits by Plan

Cowork enforces usage limits at two levels: message throughput (how many tasks you can run in a given time window) and file storage (how much data you can keep in Projects). Understanding these limits before deploying prevents surprise outages mid-project.

Cowork bills on token consumption rather than flat message counts. Each plan includes a monthly token budget, with additional tokens purchasable on demand for Pro and Team plans. A single Cowork task that reads a 50-page PDF, processes it through a subagent pipeline, and writes a summary to Drive may consume 100,000–400,000 tokens — a meaningful fraction of a Pro plan's monthly budget.

— A generous individual monthly token budget sufficient for sustained daily use. When the budget is reached, tasks queue until the budget resets (calendar month) or you purchase additional tokens. The budget resets on your billing anniversary, not on the first of the month.

— Per-seat token pool that scales with the number of active seats. The admin console shows per-user consumption so heavy users can be identified. Unused tokens in one user's allocation do not transfer to another.
— Custom token volumes negotiated in the contract. Admins can set per-team and per-user spending caps in the admin console to prevent budget overruns by any single workflow.
Each plan also limits how many parallel tasks can run simultaneously across a single account or workspace:

The wall-clock limit is the most impactful for complex workflows. A Pro user running a competitive analysis that requires 20+ web searches, two rounds of synthesis, and a Drive write may hit the 30-minute limit and have the task terminated mid-execution. Design long-running workflows to checkpoint their outputs along the way — writing intermediate results to a Project document — so a timeout does not lose all work.
Projects store two categories of data: the files and documents you upload as reference material, and the conversation/artifact history produced by tasks. Both count against your storage quota.

Uploading files to a Project stores them for retrieval, but only the portion of a file that fits within the model's active context window is processed in any single task. Very large files (hundreds of MB) may be chunked across multiple task runs. For Pro users, the 50 MB per-file limit means large PDF reports should be split before upload.

Data handling in Cowork follows Anthropic's standard privacy policy, but plan tier affects what controls you have over that handling. Understanding these differences is important for regulated industries or organizations with data governance requirements.

By default, Anthropic may use conversations to improve future models. Pro users can opt out in their account settings. Team and Enterprise workspaces have opt-out applied automatically — no configuration needed. This means all Professional and Enterprise Cowork conversations are excluded from training data by default.

All plans use TLS 1.2+ in transit and AES-256 at rest. Enterprise adds the option to use customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) for Project storage, enabling key rotation and revocation on your schedule rather than Anthropic's.
Choosing the Right Plan for Your Team
The plan decision is not purely about features — it is about the shape of your workflows and the number of people involved. Here are representative scenarios:
Solo analyst or researcher, Drive-only workflows
Projects + Drive connector sufficient; no sharing needed
2–10 person team with Slack, GitHub, or Jira workflows
Shared Projects + connector suite + scheduled tasks
SSO, audit logs, data residency required
All core features available; cheapest way to validate use case
Engineering team wanting Cowork alongside Claude Code
GitHub connector for PM-level GitHub work; Claude Code for code-level work
Plan changes take effect immediately, not at the next billing cycle. The practical consequences differ by direction:
New connectors (Slack, GitHub, Jira) become available immediately in the connector settings panel.
Existing Projects are preserved and become shareable. To share a Project, the admin must invite team members — Projects do not automatically become visible to all workspace members.
The admin console becomes available. The first user to upgrade is automatically assigned the admin role; they can then invite others at member or admin level.
Billing prorates for the remainder of the current month, then moves to the Team per-seat rate.
Shared Projects become read-only for non-owning members.
The Project owner retains full access; invited members lose the ability to run new tasks in those Projects.
Tasks that reference them will fail. Reconnecting requires re-upgrading to Team.
They are not deleted — if you re-upgrade, they resume. But they will not run while on the Pro plan.
Excess Projects above the Pro limit (20) become read-only. You must delete Projects to bring the count under the limit before new Projects can be created.

The desktop app checks for updates at launch and notifies you when a newer version is available. Updates are not forced — you can defer them — but running more than two major versions behind may produce compatibility warnings when connecting to newer Projects that use features absent in older app versions.
— Updates are delivered via the app's built-in updater. If macOS Gatekeeper blocks the update package, right-click → Open from the Downloads folder to bypass the quarantine prompt.
— Updates are installed silently in the background. The new version activates on the next app launch. No admin rights required for the update if the app was installed in the user profile directory.
— IT teams can disable auto-update and distribute specific versions via MDM (Jamf, Intune). Anthropic maintains a version download archive for enterprise customers.
— Always runs the latest version. No updates required on the client side.

A partial setup that works in most cases but fails under certain conditions is more frustrating than a completely broken install. Run this end-to-end verification sequence after initial setup to catch edge cases before they affect real work.

Open a browser DevTools console and run the following JavaScript to verify WebSocket connectivity. A successful result is a message echoed back from the server. If the connection hangs or throws a security error, WebSocket traffic is being blocked:
Create a Project named "Setup Test". Add the standing instruction: "You are a test agent. When asked to run a test, respond with PASS followed by the current task." Then ask:
Run a test. Confirm that your standing instruction is loaded and that you can complete a short task.

The response should reference the standing instruction you set, confirming Project context is loaded. If the agent does not seem to have the standing instruction, the Project is not properly active — check that you are chatting inside the Project rather than in a generic Claude session.
For each connector you intend to use, run a minimal read operation in the test Project:
"List the 5 most recently modified files in my Google Drive root." Successful response means OAuth is valid and Drive read scope is granted.
"List the Slack channels you have access to." Verifies the Slack bot has been installed to the workspace.
"List the open issues in [repo name]." Confirms the GitHub OAuth app has the correct repository access.

Create a scheduled task set to run once, 5 minutes from now, with the prompt: "Post a test message to #general on Slack: Setup verification complete." Verify the message appears in Slack at the scheduled time. If it does not, check the scheduled task logs in Settings → Scheduled Tasks for the error reason.

Cowork runs on any modern Mac or Windows machine with a stable internet connection — hardware requirements are modest because all computation is cloud-hosted. The plan tier is the key gate: Pro unlocks Projects and Drive for individual users; Team adds shared Projects, Slack, GitHub, and scheduling; Enterprise adds SSO, audit logs, and data residency. Most setup blockers are either plan tier mismatches or corporate network rules that prevent WebSocket connections or OAuth redirects. Run the end-to-end verification sequence after setup to confirm network connectivity, Project context, connector authorization, and (for Team+) scheduled task execution all work before handing the environment to users.
